Christopher Veau 09/ 05/2022 2.0 Added Batch Shipment Picking Prioritization logic. EX 1 9 – Picking Task Sequencing McLane divisions perform picking for multiple shipments simultaneously. Non-conveyable full case items are picked by stop or across multiple stops for the same shipment. McLane requires that non-conveyable full case picks to be sequenced by shipment dispatch time and stop such that picking is progressed in each shipment. To meet McLane requirement, custom logic is introduced post task creation logic to group and assign tasks a priority based on the sequence number of the shipments . The lower t he sequence number, the higher the shipment priority. Summary Information Customer Name: McLane Customer Code: MCLN Subproject Codes: TBD Change Request Number: Overview Functional Area Enhanced Additional Information User Interface (UI) N MHE N RF N Reporting N Other Y Picking Task prioritization/sequencing M cLane host bridges in a sequence number for each shipment via the ExternalSystemShipmentID tag on the shipment XML . The XML tag in turn populates the SHIPMENT . EXT_SYS_SHIPMENT_ID field in the DB . During the wave, WM looks at the sequence number s of the shipments being waved and re-prioritizes tasks based on this sequence. WM maintains a minimum priority for tasks in system code C- E19 -019 parameter 4-5 . For the first wave for the day, WM assigns tasks priority starting with this minimum and increasing by one for each subsequent batch of tasks (a batch of tasks is created for each task creation rules up to a maximum of N maximum tasks configured in C- E19 -019 parameter 1-3 ) . From 2 nd wave onwards, WM uses the maximum of picking task priority from prior waves for the day. WM uses wave numbers Assumptions B ase picking task creation rules are configured sequencing by shipment dispatch time, shipment and stop . Task creation rules are configured to break tasks for each distinct dispatch time and shipment as well as per “N” number of stops. McLane may create more than one task per shipment and dock door . Shipments have a single dispatch time (PICKUP_END _TIME) per shipment. Task groups for each IN T (e.g. INT51 - picking in case pick, INT50 – picking from active) are mutually exclusive. The logic of this extension is only valid for picking/pulling INTs directly associated to an order. Other INTs (such as replenishment) are out of scope. McLane assigns dock door s for shipment s , sequenced by dock door and virtual sequence (A, then B, then C, etc.) , before waving the associated s hipment and its orders in WMOS. McLane uses the Assign- Release Dock Door function of the Shipments UI t o associate shipments to dock doors. Multiple systemic dummy dock doors are created for the same physical dock door. For example, for physical dock door DR1, 4 virtual, systemic dock doors are created, DR1A, DR1B, DR1C and DR1D . WM allows a single shipment to be assigned to a dock door at a time. McLane operations assigns dock door s for shipments processed by the same wave to a set of dock door s with the same suffix. For example, the first batch of shipments are assigned to only dock doors ending with “A ”. All shipments are assigned with ambient outbound staging locations before wave . The naming of outbound staging location follows same as dock doors , STG1A , STG1B , STG1 C and STG1 D . At the time of task creation, picking tasks for a task batch are created with the same task priority. Base WM sorts and assigns tasks by ascending t ask release time when tasks have been assigned the same task priority . After tasks are created, McLane may choose to increase or decrease the priority of a task using base WM task priority change functions . Changing the priority of a task affects the sequence of task assignment in WM such that the intended sequencing logic of this extension may no longer be valid. The custom task re-prioritization logic of this extension is called after Task Creation Rules create tasks using the base task creation/prioritization logic . Other tasking features (task release, task priority rules, etc.) are out of scope. McLane runs task creation rules with the wave. All other task creation triggers are out of scope. McLane does not utilize pack wave functionality. WM continues to increment the task priority for tasks associated with the logic of this extension over the course of the day. Operations is responsible for managing and assigning tasks to ensure that the work prioritized by this extension is completed. McLane waves full orders with each wave. Partial allocation is disabled. If any line item for an order is short, the order is dropped from the allocation process and no tasks are created. Orders are assigned to shipments before tasking through the SWFO interface into WMOS . McLane creates tasks in a Locked (STAT_CODE = 5 ) status or Released (STAT_CODE = 10) status . Other task creation statuses are out of scope. Tasks are released through dependent task release functionality. WM prioritizes tasks in ascending, numeric priority sequence – tasks with priority 1 ar 5 e the first tasks assigned following by priority 2, then priority 3. Etc. Updates to the minimum task priority value in system code C- E19 -019 parameter 4-5 are not reflected in the task prioritization logic of this extension until the first wave for the next day . McLane wave numbers are assigned in the following format YYYYMMDDXXX ( where ‘ Y YYY’ = the year, ‘M M ’ = the month, ‘D D ’ = the day, and ‘ XXX ’ = a counter that increases by one for each wave run on the same day ) and the date is changed for first wave of the day . McLane operations is responsible for completing work for any given day in a timely manner such that tasks created at the end of one day do not fall behind the first tasks created the next day. SQL queries in this document are meant to be samples and are not necessarily used in any code. If a wave is undone, the priority of those tasks are still taken into consideration for future waves . For tasks executed via Label Picking, printing is customized in EX21 to handle sending the print requests to the printer in priority sequence. These are separated by oLPN Label Print and Sequencing rules. Chase tasks belong to their own individual task groups. Pal lets and Carts are not staged for loading in the same area Dependencies Code Dependencies Interface Dependencies Interface Field Requirement P latform Information Solution: Solution Platform: Solution Version: Warehouse Management Open Systems Oracle/ Tomcat /RHEL 201 9 Proces s Flow Diagram Process Steps : Tasks are created by the wave as part of the base WM task creation process. At the end of task creation, WM checks to see EX19 is enabled. If yes, WM proceeds with custom logic. WM checks to see if any of the tasks associated with the wave are enabled for EX19 re-prioritization. If yes, WM proceeds with custom logic. WM checks whether any previous EX-19 enabled tasks have been created (and prioritized) for the day. If previous EX19 tasks have been created for the day, WM sets the priority counter as maximum of picking task for the day +1. Otherwise, WM sets the priority counter to the minimum value using C-E19-019 parameter 4 - 5 . WM identifies a batch of tasks to be prioritized based on the maximum number of tasks per batch configured in system code C- E19 -019 parameter 1-3 . Batches are broken by the task creation rules . WM assigns the tasks a priority equal to the current counter and then increments the counter by one. If further EX19 need to be prioritized for the wave, WM returns to step 5 and repeats until no more tasks are available. After all tasks are updated, WM proceeds with the next step in the wave. UI Not in scope for this extension. MHE Not in scope for this extension. RF Not in scope for this extension. Process Change/Addition Task Prioritization and Sequencing Enablement The following section describes the custom configuration required to enable this section of the extension. First, the global modifications flag must be enabled. This is controlled through the custom system code C000 code id 000 parameter byte 1-1. This parameter is configured as a Y/N flag. If the flag is set to ‘Y’, then WMOS continues to the next enablement validation. Otherwise, if this flag is set to ‘N’, the global extension control is turned off, and only base processing will occur. Next , the extension specific global flag must be enabled. This is controlled through the custom system code C000 code id EX1 9 byte 1-1. This parameter is configured as a Y/N flag. If the flag is set to ‘Y’, then WMOS continues to the next validation . Otherwise, if the flag is set to ‘N’, the extension specific control for EX1 9 is turned off, and no custom EX1 9 processing will occur. Finally, the wave template specific flag must be enabled. This is controlled the wave miscellaneous instruction code 5 ( B-784 ) . If the code id is setup as “19”, then WMOS continues to the custom processing below. Otherwise, the extension specific control for EX19 is turned off, and no custom EX19 processing will occur. Validations There are no custom validations added as part of the logic of this extension. Updates Following custom logic is processed only if EX19 is enabled . Otherwise, WM exits custom logic, and no custom updates occur. WM first determines the START_TASK_PRIORITY for the wave. If there are prior waves for the day with EX19 enabled ( WAVE_PARM.SPL_INSTR_CODE_5 = “19” and date of wave numbers = date of current wave number), WM uses the maximum+1 of task priority of all picking tasks (INT2, INT50 and INT51) of all prior waves for the day with EX19 enabled. Otherwise, WMOS uses the value of C-E19-019 parameter 4 1 - 5 3 . Note: First 8 characters of picking wave number is used to determine date. WM executes the custom logic f or each picking task creation rule (INT2, INT50 and INT 51 ) after the task s are created for that rule . WM processes tasks for one task creation rule across all shipments for a wave . WM considers only INT 2 , INT50 or INT51 task creation rules and ignores other task creation rules. After task creation, WM processes all tasks for one task creation rule at a time and assigns priority based on the shipment sequence number. For example: Shipment Shipment Sequence # bridged from host Shipment A 1 Shipment B 20 Shipment C 30 During wave and a fter task creation, WM evaluate task creation rules (In this scenario, WM looks at all the tasks associated to the dry case pallet pick task creation rule first and then move on to the dry case cart pick task creation rule) and assign a task priority based on the shipment sequence number bridged in from host . Starting priority is configured as 1 . Task ID Task Type INT Associated Shipment Shipment Sequence # Priority 1 Dry case pallet pick INT 51 Shipment A 1 1 2 Dry case pallet pick INT 51 Shipment C 30 3 3 Dry case pallet pick INT 51 Shipment B 20 2 4 Dry case cart pick INT 51 Shipment A 1 1 5 Dry case cart pick INT 51 Shipment C 30 3 6 Dry case cart pick INT 51 Shipment B 20 2 WM sorts all picking tasks for shipments by task creation rule and s hipment sequence number . Once all the tasks within a task creation rule are processed, WM continues with the next task create rule again starting with START_TASK_PRIORITY . The TASK_BATCH_SIZE for re-prioritization is determined by C-E19-019 parameter 1 4 - 3 5 . F or every TASK_BATCH_SIZE number task s from the sorted list WM assigns new task priority starting with START_TASK_PRIORITY. Outputs Not in scope for this section . Chase Wave Picking Task Prioritization Additional logic is added to the base Picking Task Server logic to ensure that a tasks priority is corelated to the quantity times unit price on the picking task (unit x dollar amount) . EX19 introduces new configured value that MCLN configured as the maximum dollar value for prioritization. The higher the dollar amount of the picking task the higher the priority of the task . WMOS is customized to ensure tasks are released by wave time when a tie in dollar amount occurs. Enablement The following section describes the custom flags required to enable this portion of the extension. First, the global modifications flag must be enabled. This is controlled through the custom system code C000 code id 000 parameter byte 1-1. This parameter is configured as a Y/N flag. If the flag is set to ‘Y’, then WMOS continues to the next enablement validation. Otherwise, if this flag is set to ‘N’, the global extension control is turned off, and only base processing will occur. Second, the extension specific global flag must be enabled. This is controlled through the custom system code C000 code id E X 19 byte 1-1. This parameter is configured as a Y/N flag. If the flag is set to ‘Y’, then WMOS continues to the custom processing below. Otherwise, if the flag is set to ‘N’, the extension specific control for EX 19 is turned off, and no custom E X 19 processing will occur. Third , the extension specific task type must be enabled. This is controlled through the system code 59 0 custom byte 1 00 -1 00 . This parameter is configured as a Y/N flag. If the flag is set to ‘Y’, then WMOS continues to the custom processing below. Otherwise, if the flag is set to ‘N’, the extension specific control for EX 19 is turned off, and no custom EX 19 processing will occur. Forth , the extension specific task type must be enabled. This is controlled through the system code C E19 code id E19 byte 7 - 11 . This parameter is configured as a n edit box and is set to a value configured by MCLN , then WMOS continues to the custom processing below. Otherwise, if the flag is ‘N ULL ’, the extension specific control for maximum value is turned off, and no custom EX 19 processing will occur. Lastly, the Picking Task Prioritization flag must be enabled. This is controlled through custom system code C- E 19 code id 01 byte 6 - 6 . This parameter is configured as a Y/N flag. If the flag is set to ‘Y’, then WMOS continues to the custom processing below. Otherwise, if the flag is set to ‘N’, the extension specific control for the custom Picking Task Prioritization logic is turned off, and no custom task release processing will occur. Validations There are no custom validations added as part of the logic of this extension. Updates Following custom logic is processed only if EX19 is enabled. Otherwise, WM exits custom logic, and no custom updates occur. WM executes the custom logic for each picking task after the tasks are created. WM processes tasks and counts the total number of units on all the details per task . Based on the quantity, WM assigns a priority to the task where the higher the quantity of the task the higher the priority. WM calculates priority using the following equation (6000 is used as example max dollar value) : SUM( TD.QTY_ALLOC * IW.UNIT _PRICE) = Chase Task Dollar Value IF; Chase Task Dollar Value ≥ 6000, then Priority = 1 Else Priority = CEIL( 1 - Chase Task Dollar Value / 6000) * 1000 WM leverages wave/release time to release tasks in the order of which they are waved . WM invokes this logic when a second chase wave is run before the initial chase waves tasks are complete and two tasks have the exact same number of units. WM executes the custom logic for each picking task creation rule (INT2, INT50 and INT51) after the tasks are created for that rule. WM processes tasks for one task creation rule across all shipments for a wave. WM considers only INT2, INT50 or INT51 task creation rules and ignores other task creation rules. Examples Scenario 1: Picking tasks priority set by highest dollar quantity. 3 tasks are created by the wave. Tasks are given highest priority by the quantity on the picking task. TASK1 – Priority = (( 9000 +376) ) > 6000 Prty = 1 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 900 900* 10.00 = 9000 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 100 100 *3.76 = 376 TASK 2 – Priority = ( 1-( ( 2565 +376) /6000 ) ) * 1000 Prty = 509 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 5 00 900* 2.85 = 2565 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 100 100 *3.76 = 376 TASK3 – Priority = ( 1-( ( 325.5 + 88 )/6000 ) ) * 1000 Prty = 931 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 3 0 30 * 10.85 = 325.5 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 5 0 50 * 1 .76 = 88 Output: Task1 has the highest priority due to it being the highest dollar amount of the three picking tasks. Task2 is next highest and then Task3. Scenario 2: Second chase wave is run before first chase wave tasks are complete. 3 tasks are created by the wave. Tasks are given highest priority by the quantity on the picking task. Chase Wave 1 TASK1 – Priority = ((9000+376)) > 6000 Prty = 1 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 900 900* 10.00 = 9000 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 100 100 *3.76 = 376 TASK2 – Priority = (1-((2565+376)/6000 ))* 1000 Prty = 509 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 5 00 900* 2.85 = 2562 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 100 100 *3.76 = 376 TASK3 – Priority = (1-((325.5+88)/6000 ))* 1000 Prty = 931 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 3 0 30 * 10.85 = 325.5 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 5 0 50 * 1 .76 = 88 Output: Task1 has the highest priority due to it being the highest quantity of the three picking tasks. Task2 is next highest and then Task3. Chase Wave 2 TASK1 – Priority = ((8000+376)) > 6000 Prty = 1 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 8 00 8 00* 10.00 = 8000 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 100 100 *3.76 = 376 TASK2 – Priority = (1-(( 1710 +376)/6000 ))* 1000 Prty = 652 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 6 00 6 00* 2.85 = 1710 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 100 100 *3.76 = 376 TASK3 – Priority = (1-((325.5+88)/6000 ))* 1000 Prty = 894 Task Detail Location Item Quantity Dollar Value 1 LOCN1 ITEMA 5 0 5 0 * 10.85 = 542.5 2 LOCN2 ITEMB 5 0 50 * 1 .76 = 88 Output: Task1 has the highest priority due to it being the highest dollar amount of the three picking tasks for this wave , priority is still 1 due to it being the worth more than the configured value amount in C-E19-VAL . Task 1 is higher in priority than task 2 on first chase wave due to higher dollar amount . Task 2 is next highest and then Task 3 . Outputs N/A Batch Shipment Picking Task Prioritization The following logic, w hen enabled, introduces some additions to the t ask reprioritization logic in section 7.1 . Similarly to the section 7.1 logic WM reprioritize s the tas ks after task creation. The prioritization in this section is primarily driven by three parameters. The first is a new parameter introduced on the Task Type system code B-590 which determines which tasks to assess in the below logic. If system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “Y” for the task type, then it will then follow custom logic detailed in 7.3. Else i f the system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “ N ” for the task type, then it will use the prior exist ing EX19 custom logic detailed in 7.1 . WM is customized to invoke logic 7.1 before logic 7.3 for custom updates. The second is the number of shipments that are considered a batch. Th is determines how many shipme nts and their associated tasks WM takes into consideration when creating the task sequence. The last paramet er is used to determine, for a certain task type on a shipment, what percentage of that task type is to be picked first. So for a certain batch of shipments and task type , WM take s into consideration the tasks within the percentage a nd sequence s them so that one task is picked per shipment in accordance to the shipment sequence number . This is repeated till all the tasks within the task type percentage are sequenced for all the shipments within the batch. Then the next task type is assessed for the batch of shipments using the same logic. If system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “Y” for the task type, then it will then follow custom logic detailed in 7.3. Else if the system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “N” for the task type, then it will use the prior existing EX19 custom logic detailed in 7.1. WM is customized to invoke logic 7.1 before logic 7.3 for custom updates. 4 1 - 5 3 . WM then checks C-E19-019 parameter 13-1 4 to determine th e number of shipments that will be part of the b atch whose tasks will be assessed for reprioritization . WM assesses the shipment s in shipment sequence number order . For XX number of shipments WM gathers all the INT2, INT50 or INT51 tasks whose Task Type have been flagged to be part of this logic. For this WM looks at S -590 Byte 1 9 -1 9 to be set to ‘Y’. For each shipment WM first sequences the Task Type s by Task pr iority, then withing each task type WM seq uence s the tasks by TASK ID ascending WM then checks C-E19-019 parameter 15-17 to determine for a shipment which tasks within a task type are to be assessed for re-sequencing. C-E19-019 parameter 15-17 is set as a percentage threshold. The threshold is based off number of packs (cases) . WM d etermines which tasks to reprioritize by calculat ing the total number of packs for all the tasks within the same task ty pe on a single shipment and then multiplying that by the threshold percentage. The resulting number of packs is rounded to the next whole number. Based off the number of packs, WM selects the appropriate number of tasks , which fulfill the pack quantity, in the sequence that was created in the previous step . WM does this for each shipment within the batch of shipme n ts , the resulting tasks found for each shipment for the same task type is considered as the batch of task s NOTE: If only part of a task is needed to fulfill the pack quantity, then the entirety of the task is included in the first batch. The first batch of tasks are then re-prioritized with the following logic. The tasks are prioritized based off the shipment sequence number and the task sequence determined in previous step . The task priorities sta rt with the determined priority in the first step above an d are i ncreased +1 for each task. This is carried out until all tasks in the first batch have been prioritized. Once the first batch of tasks are assessed, t his process is repeated for each task type in order of task creation priority. Once all eligible task types are assessed, WM then determi nes the next group of shipments to be assessed using the C-E19-019 parameter 13-14 to determine the number of shipments . In addition to the se next shipments the shipments from the previous batch are to also be included in the logic if any tasks remain to be re-sequenced for those shipments . The process is then repeated with the new batch of shipments. Starting with the step whe re the Task Types are sequenced by Task Priority. This logic will continue looping until all shipments have been assessed and all tasks have been reprioritized. Christopher Veau 09/ 05/2022 2.0 Added Batch Shipment Picking Prioritization logic. EX 1 9 – Picking Task Sequencing McLane divisions perform picking for multiple shipments simultaneously. Non-conveyable full case items are picked by stop or across multiple stops for the same shipment. McLane requires that non-conveyable full case picks to be sequenced by shipment dispatch time and stop such that picking is progressed in each shipment. To meet McLane requirement, custom logic is introduced post task creation logic to group and assign tasks a priority based on the sequence number of the shipments . The lower t he sequence number, the higher the shipment priority. During the wave, WM looks at the sequence number s of the shipments being waved and re-prioritizes tasks based on this sequence. WM maintains a minimum priority for tasks in system code C- E19 -019 parameter 4-5 . For the first wave for the day, WM assigns tasks priority starting with this minimum and increasing by one for each subsequent batch of tasks (a batch of tasks is created for each task creation rules up to a maximum of N maximum tasks configured in C- E19 -019 parameter 1-3 ) . From 2 nd wave onwards, WM uses the maximum of picking task priority from prior waves for the day. WM uses wave numbers Assumptions B ase picking task creation rules are configured sequencing by shipment dispatch time, shipment and stop . Task creation rules are configured to break tasks for each distinct dispatch time and shipment as well as per “N” number of stops. McLane may create more than one task per shipment and dock door . Shipments have a single dispatch time (PICKUP_END _TIME) per shipment. Task groups for each IN T (e.g. INT51 - picking in case pick, INT50 – picking from active) are mutually exclusive. The logic of this extension is only valid for picking/pulling INTs directly associated to an order. Other INTs (such as replenishment) are out of scope. McLane assigns dock door s for shipment s , sequenced by dock door and virtual sequence (A, then B, then C, etc.) , before waving the associated s hipment and its orders in WMOS. McLane uses the Assign- Release Dock Door function of the Shipments UI t o associate shipments to dock doors. Multiple systemic dummy dock doors are created for the same physical dock door. For example, for physical dock door DR1, 4 virtual, systemic dock doors are created, DR1A, DR1B, DR1C and DR1D . WM allows a single shipment to be assigned to a dock door at a time. McLane operations assigns dock door s for shipments processed by the same wave to a set of dock door s with the same suffix. For example, the first batch of shipments are assigned to only dock doors ending with “A ”. All shipments are assigned with ambient outbound staging locations before wave . The naming of outbound staging location follows same as dock doors , STG1A , STG1B , STG1 C and STG1 D . At the time of task creation, picking tasks for a task batch are created with the same task priority. Base WM sorts and assigns tasks by ascending t ask release time when tasks have been assigned the same task priority .

ing location follows same as dock doors , STG1A , STG1B , STG1 C and STG1 D . At the time of task creation, picking tasks for a task batch are created with the same task priority. Base WM sorts and assigns tasks by ascending t ask release time when tasks have been assigned the same task priority .

After tasks are created, McLane may choose to increase or decrease the priority of a task using base WM task priority change functions . Changing the priority of a task affects the sequence of task assignment in WM such that the intended sequencing logic of this extension may no longer be valid. The custom task re-prioritization logic of this extension is called after Task Creation Rules create tasks using the base task creation/prioritization logic . Other tasking features (task release, task priority rules, etc.) are out of scope. McLane runs task creation rules with the wave. All other task creation triggers are out of scope. McLane does not utilize pack wave functionality. WM continues to increment the task priority for tasks associated with the logic of this extension over the course of the day. Operations is responsible for managing and assigning tasks to ensure that the work prioritized by this extension is completed. McLane waves full orders with each wave. Partial allocation is disabled. If any line item for an order is short, the order is dropped from the allocation process and no tasks are created. Orders are assigned to shipments before tasking through the SWFO interface into WMOS . McLane creates tasks in a Locked (STAT_CODE = 5 ) status or Released (STAT_CODE = 10) status . Other task creation statuses are out of scope. Tasks are released through dependent task release functionality. WM prioritizes tasks in ascending, numeric priority sequence – tasks with priority 1 ar 5 e the first tasks assigned following by priority 2, then priority 3. Etc. Updates to the minimum task priority value in system code C- E19 -019 parameter 4-5 are not reflected in the task prioritization logic of this extension until the first wave for the next day . McLane wave numbers are assigned in the following format YYYYMMDDXXX ( where ‘ Y YYY’ = the year, ‘M M ’ = the month, ‘D D ’ = the day, and ‘ XXX ’ = a counter that increases by one for each wave run on the same day ) and the date is changed for first wave of the day . McLane operations is responsible for completing work for any given day in a timely manner such that tasks created at the end of one day do not fall behind the first tasks created the next day. At the end of task creation, WM checks to see EX19 is enabled. If yes, WM proceeds with custom logic. WM checks to see if any of the tasks associated with the wave are enabled for EX19 re-prioritization. If yes, WM proceeds with custom logic. WM checks whether any previous EX-19 enabled tasks have been created (and prioritized) for the day. If previous EX19 tasks have been created for the day, WM sets the priority counter as maximum of picking task for the day +1. Otherwise, WM sets the priority counter to the minimum value using C-E19-019 parameter 4 - 5 . WM identifies a batch of tasks to be prioritized based on the maximum number of tasks per batch configured in system code C- E19 -019 parameter 1-3 . Batches are broken by the task creation rules . WM assigns the tasks a priority equal to the current counter and then increments the counter by one. If further EX19 need to be prioritized for the wave, WM returns to step 5 and repeats until no more tasks are available. After all tasks are updated, WM proceeds with the next step in the wave. WM processes tasks and counts the total number of units on all the details per task . Outputs N/A Batch Shipment Picking Task Prioritization The following logic, w hen enabled, introduces some additions to the t ask reprioritization logic in section 7.1 . Similarly to the section 7.1 logic WM reprioritize s the tas ks after task creation. The prioritization in this section is primarily driven by three parameters. The first is a new parameter introduced on the Task Type system code B-590 which determines which tasks to assess in the below logic. If system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “Y” for the task type, then it will then follow custom logic detailed in 7.3. Else i f the system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “ N ” for the task type, then it will use the prior exist ing EX19 custom logic detailed in 7.1 . WM is customized to invoke logic 7.1 before logic 7.3 for custom updates. The second is the number of shipments that are considered a batch. Th is determines how many shipme nts and their associated tasks WM takes into consideration when creating the task sequence. The last paramet er is used to determine, for a certain task type on a shipment, what percentage of that task type is to be picked first. So for a certain batch of shipments and task type , WM take s into consideration the tasks within the percentage a nd sequence s them so that one task is picked per shipment in accordance to the shipment sequence number . This is repeated till all the tasks within the task type percentage are sequenced for all the shipments within the batch. Then the next task type is assessed for the batch of shipments using the same logic. If system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “Y” for the task type, then it will then follow custom logic detailed in 7.3. Else if the system code B-590 parameter byte 19-19 is set to “N” for the task type, then it will use the prior existing EX19 custom logic detailed in 7.1. WM is customized to invoke logic 7.1 before logic 7.3 for custom updates. 4 1 - 5 3 . WM then checks C-E19-019 parameter 13-1 4 to determine th e number of shipments that will be part of the b atch whose tasks will be assessed for reprioritization . WM assesses the shipment s in shipment sequence number order . For XX number of shipments WM gathers all the INT2, INT50 or INT51 tasks whose Task Type have been flagged to be part of this logic.

For this WM looks at S -590 Byte 1 9 -1 9 to be set to ‘Y’. For each shipment WM first sequences the Task Type s by Task pr iority, then withing each task type WM seq uence s the tasks by TASK ID ascending WM then checks C-E19-019 parameter 15-17 to determine for a shipment which tasks within a task type are to be assessed for re-sequencing. C-E19-019 parameter 15-17 is set as a percentage threshold. The threshold is based off number of packs (cases) . WM d etermines which tasks to reprioritize by calculat ing the total number of packs for all the tasks within the same task ty pe on a single shipment and then multiplying that by the threshold percentage. The resulting number of packs is rounded to the next whole number. Based off the number of packs, WM selects the appropriate number of tasks , which fulfill the pack quantity, in the sequence that was created in the previous step . WM does this for each shipment within the batch of shipme n ts , the resulting tasks found for each shipment for the same task type is considered as the batch of task s NOTE: If only part of a task is needed to fulfill the pack quantity, then the entirety of the task is included in the first batch. The first batch of tasks are then re-prioritized with the following logic. The tasks are prioritized based off the shipment sequence number and the task sequence determined in previous step . The task priorities sta rt with the determined priority in the first step above an d are i ncreased +1 for each task. This is carried out until all tasks in the first batch have been prioritized. Once the first batch of tasks are assessed, t his process is repeated for each task type in order of task creation priority. Once all eligible task types are assessed, WM then determi nes the next group of shipments to be assessed using the C-E19-019 parameter 13-14 to determine the number of shipments . In addition to the se next shipments the shipments from the previous batch are to also be included in the logic if any tasks remain to be re-sequenced for those shipments . The process is then repeated with the new batch of shipments. Starting with the step whe re the Task Types are sequenced by Task Priority. This logic will continue looping until all shipments have been assessed and all tasks have been reprioritized.
